cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out which statement is sarcastic between the two options provided. First, let's look at each option carefully. Option (A): "You're welcome my dear friend. Always glad to feed the elephants, that woefully endangered species." Option (B): "You're welcome my dear friend. Always glad to feed the trolls, that woefully endangered species." The first part of both statements is the same: "You're welcome my dear friend." This is a polite response, so the sarcasm, if present, must be in the second part of the sentence. Now, let's focus on the second part of each statement. In option (A), the speaker says they're "glad to feed the elephants, that woefully endangered species." Elephants are indeed real animals and are actually considered endangered in many parts of the world. So if someone is feeding elephants, that would typically be a positive thing, and referring to them as "woefully endangered" seems to be a genuine statement. Therefore, this part of the sentence might not be sarcastic. In option (B), the speaker says they're "glad to feed the trolls, that woefully endangered species." Trolls in this context are not the mythical creatures but likely refer to internet trolls - people who post provocative or inflammatory messages online. The phrase "woefully endangered species" is being used sarcastically here because internet trolls are not endangered at all; in fact, they are quite common and often seen as a nuisance. By calling them a "woefully endangered species," the speaker is being ironic or sarcastic, as it's the opposite of the truth.
